Industrial food production and distribution systems ensure year-round availability of diverse food
commodities. Cold chain technology (refrigeration — a product of industrialization) reduces food spoilage
and related foodborne illness. Modern food processing has extended shelf life and made nutritious foods
accessible.
NEGATIVE IMPACTS ON COMMUNITY HEALTH
1. Environmental Pollution
Air Pollution: Industrial emissions (factories, power plants, vehicles) release particulate matter (PM2.5,
PM10), sulfur dioxide, nitrogen oxides, and volatile organic compounds. These cause: Respiratory diseases
(asthma, COPD, bronchitis, lung cancer), Cardiovascular diseases (ischemic heart disease, stroke),
Developmental problems in children (low birth weight, cognitive impairment). WHO estimates that air
pollution causes 7 million premature deaths annually globally.
Water Pollution: Industrial effluents (heavy metals, chemicals, dyes), agricultural runoff (pesticides,
fertilizers), and untreated sewage contaminate water bodies. Consequences: Waterborne diseases
(cholera, typhoid, hepatitis A), Chemical toxicity (lead, mercury, arsenic poisoning), Methemoglobinemia in
children (nitrate contamination).
Soil Pollution: Industrial chemicals, heavy metals, and persistent organic pollutants (POPs) contaminate
soil, entering the food chain and causing chronic toxicity, cancer, and endocrine disruption.
For Exam Use Only | Prepared with Gold Standard Content

Health
Noise Pollution: Industrial machinery and urban traffic generate noise exceeding safe limits (>85 dB).
Consequences: Noise-induced hearing loss (occupational hazard), Hypertension and cardiovascular
disease (chronic noise stress), Sleep disturbances, Cognitive impairment in children.
2. Occupational Hazards
Industrial workplaces expose workers to unique health risks:
● Dust exposure: Silica dust (miners, quarry workers) → Silicosis; Coal dust → Coal Worker's
Pneumoconiosis (Black Lung disease); Asbestos → Asbestosis, mesothelioma, lung cancer.
● Chemical exposure: Organic solvents, benzene → Leukemia, liver damage; Pesticides →
Neurotoxicity, reproductive harm; Heavy metals (lead, mercury) → Neurological damage.
● Physical hazards: Extreme heat/cold, vibration (HAVS — Hand-Arm Vibration Syndrome), ionizing
radiation (cancer risk), electrical hazards.
● Ergonomic hazards: Repetitive strain injuries (carpal tunnel syndrome), low back pain,
musculoskeletal disorders.
● Accidents and injuries: Factory accidents, falls, machinery injuries — leading cause of occupational
mortality.
3. Lifestyle-Related Non-Communicable Diseases (NCDs)
Urbanization drives profound lifestyle changes that fuel the NCD epidemic:
● Physical inactivity: Office jobs, motorized transport, and sedentary leisure → Obesity, type 2
diabetes, hypertension, cardiovascular disease, certain cancers.
● Unhealthy diet: Increased consumption of ultra-processed foods, fast food, refined carbohydrates,
saturated fats, and sugar → Obesity, metabolic syndrome, NCDs.
● Tobacco and alcohol: Marketing of tobacco and alcohol to urban populations; stress-driven
consumption → Lung cancer, COPD, liver cirrhosis, accidents.
● Stress: Competitive urban work environment, job insecurity, long commutes, social isolation →
Hypertension, depression, anxiety, substance abuse.
4. Overcrowding and Communicable Diseases in Slums
Rapid unplanned urbanization creates urban slums characterized by extreme overcrowding, inadequate
housing, poor sanitation, and lack of clean water. This creates ideal conditions for: Airborne infections
(tuberculosis — urban slums are the epicenter of the TB epidemic; influenza; measles), Skin infections
(scabies, dermatophytosis — due to overcrowding and poor hygiene), Vector-borne diseases (malaria,
dengue — due to stagnant water and poor waste management in slums), Diarrheal diseases (due to poor
sanitation and contaminated water).
5. Mental Health Consequences
Urbanization and industrialization are associated with significantly higher rates of mental illness: Social
isolation in large anonymous cities vs. tight-knit rural communities; Stress from work pressure, financial
insecurity, and urban competitive culture; Disruption of traditional social support systems (joint family
breakdown) when people migrate; Higher rates of depression, anxiety disorders, schizophrenia, and
substance use disorders in urban populations. Mental health services remain underfunded despite high
urban need.
6. Social Fragmentation and Health Inequity
Industrial capitalism creates social stratification, with wealthy urban elites and impoverished urban poor
often living in the same city but experiencing vastly different health outcomes. Rural-urban migration breaks
up traditional joint family structures, leaving the elderly without care in villages and young migrants without
social support in cities. Social cohesion declines in large urban areas, reducing the protective health effects
of community networks.
URBAN vs. RURAL HEALTH COMPARISON
For Exam Use Only | Prepared with Gold Standard Content

Health
Health Indicator Urban Areas Rural Areas
Dominant diseases NCDs (CVD, diabetes, obesity, Communicable diseases,
cancer) malnutrition
Air quality Poor (industry, vehicles) Generally better
Healthcare access Good (hospitals, specialists) Limited (primary care only)
Sanitation Generally better (but not in slums) Often poor
Mental health Higher stress, depression rates Better social support, less stress
Occupational hazards Industrial, chemical, sedentary work Agricultural (pesticides, injuries)
Food security Diverse supply, but food deserts in Seasonal variations, better fresh
slums produce
PUBLIC HEALTH RESPONSES
● Environmental regulations: Pollution control laws, emission standards, industrial effluent treatment.
● Occupational health legislation: Workplace safety standards, PPE provision, health surveillance of
workers.
● Urban planning: Green spaces, cycling infrastructure, walkable neighborhoods, smoke-free zones.
● Slum upgrading programs: Improved sanitation, clean water supply, waste management in informal
settlements.
● Health promotion: NCD prevention campaigns, tobacco control, physical activity promotion.
● Mental health services: Community mental health centers, employer wellness programs, crisis
helplines.
● Social safety nets: Unemployment benefits, affordable housing, food security programs.
EXAM TIP: Key concept: Epidemiological Transition — the shift from communicable diseases to NCDs as
the dominant cause of morbidity and mortality. This is driven by industrialization and urbanization. Omran's
3 stages: Age of pestilence & famine → Age of receding pandemics → Age of degenerative & man-made
diseases.
CONCLUSION: Industrialization and urbanization have generated enormous wealth and infrastructure that
has improved health in many ways. However, they have simultaneously created environmental,
occupational, and lifestyle-related health burdens. The challenge for public health is to harness the benefits
of economic development while mitigating its negative health consequences through evidence-based
policies and multi-sectoral action.
Q5. What are the social factors influencing treatment seeking behaviour?
| Frequency: High Priority
INTRODUCTION
Treatment-seeking behaviour (TSB) refers to the sequence of actions undertaken by an individual or group
when faced with a health problem, including the decision to recognize illness, seek care, choose the type
of provider, and adhere to treatment. It is not a single decision but a complex process influenced by multiple
interacting social, cultural, economic, and psychological factors.
For Exam Use Only | Prepared with Gold Standard Content

Health
Understanding treatment-seeking behaviour is essential for public health because delays or avoidance of
appropriate care lead to: progression of disease, increased complications, higher mortality, continued
transmission of communicable diseases, and increased healthcare costs.
THEORETICAL FRAMEWORK: THE HEALTH BELIEF MODEL (HBM)
The Health Belief Model (Rosenstock, 1966) explains that people seek treatment based on their beliefs
about disease and healthcare. The key constructs are:
● Perceived Susceptibility: 'Am I at risk for this disease?' High perceived risk → more likely to seek
care.
● Perceived Severity: 'How serious is this disease?' High perceived severity → more care-seeking.
● Perceived Benefits: 'Will treatment help me?' High perceived benefits → care-seeking.
● Perceived Barriers: 'What will stop me?' High barriers (cost, distance, stigma) → delayed care-
seeking.
● Cues to Action: Triggers (symptoms, reminder from family/health worker, media campaign) →
prompt care-seeking.
● Self-Efficacy: 'Can I take action?' High self-efficacy → proactive health behaviour.
SOCIAL FACTORS INFLUENCING TREATMENT-SEEKING BEHAVIOUR
1. Socioeconomic Status (SES)
SES is one of the most powerful determinants of treatment-seeking behaviour. Low income creates direct
financial barriers: inability to afford consultation fees, laboratory investigations, medicines, or transport to
health facilities. Poverty also leads to competing priorities — spending limited resources on food rather
than healthcare. Indirect costs (loss of wages from time off work for consultations) further deter care-
seeking among daily wage earners. High SES individuals benefit from health insurance, awareness of
disease, and proximity to quality health services. Social class differences in TSB explain much of the health
inequity observed globally.
2. Education and Health Literacy
Education shapes health literacy — the ability to obtain, process, and understand health information to
make appropriate health decisions. Low education leads to: Failure to recognize symptoms as serious,
delayed presentation; Inability to read health education materials or prescription instructions; Poor
understanding of disease etiology and the importance of completing treatment courses; Reliance on
traditional or informal sources of health information. High education and health literacy lead to earlier care-
seeking, better adherence, and more effective use of preventive services.
3. Cultural Beliefs and Traditional Practices
Culture profoundly shapes how illness is perceived, explained, and responded to. Cultural belief systems
influence TSB through: Explanatory models of illness — attributing disease to supernatural causes
(witchcraft, karma, God's will) leads to seeking religious healing rather than biomedical care; Use of
traditional healers — faith healers, ayurvedic/unani practitioners, bonesetters are often the first point of
contact, causing delays in reaching appropriate care; Self-medication with herbs, home remedies, or over-
the-counter medicines before seeking formal care; Dietary taboos during illness that may worsen nutritional
status. Example: In many communities, mental illness is attributed to spirit possession, leading families to
take patients to traditional healers rather than psychiatrists.
4. Stigma
Stigma is a powerful social barrier to treatment-seeking. Disease-associated stigma leads to: Concealment
of illness to avoid social discrimination; Avoidance of health facilities where diagnosis might be disclosed;
Discontinuation of treatment to avoid being identified as ill; Social isolation of patients that reduces the
support available to encourage care. Conditions with the highest stigma burden include: HIV/AIDS,
tuberculosis, mental illness, leprosy, sexually transmitted infections, and epilepsy. Addressing stigma
For Exam Use Only | Prepared with Gold Standard Content

Health
through community education, patient-centred communication, and reducing visibility of diagnosis (e.g.,
confidential testing) is essential to improving TSB.
5. Gender and Social Roles
Gender influences TSB through multiple mechanisms:
Women's TSB: In patriarchal societies, women often lack autonomy to decide to seek care independently,
requiring husband or in-laws' permission. Financial dependence on male family members restricts their
ability to afford or access care. However, women are generally more likely to seek care for preventive
services and child health than men.
Men's TSB: Cultural norms of masculinity in many societies discourage men from admitting illness ('real
men don't get sick'). Men are more likely to delay care-seeking, present late with advanced disease, and
have lower utilization of preventive services. This explains higher mortality in men for many conditions
(cancer, cardiovascular disease) despite lower morbidity.
Maternal health: Female education, empowerment, and decision-making autonomy are the strongest
predictors of maternal and child health service utilization globally.
6. Social Support Systems
Strong social support from family, friends, and community significantly facilitates care-seeking. Family
members often serve as: 'Illness recognizers' — identifying that a person needs care; Decision-makers —
deciding whether and where to seek care; Caregivers — accompanying the patient and providing transport,
financial assistance, and emotional support. Conversely, social isolation (single-person households,
migrants away from family) is associated with delayed care-seeking and poor adherence. Community social
norms (e.g., communities where going to hospital is normalized and encouraged) increase healthcare
utilization.
7. Geographic Accessibility
Physical distance to health facilities is a critical barrier, particularly in rural and remote areas. The 'three
delays model' (Thaddeus & Maine, 1994), originally developed for maternal mortality, applies broadly to
TSB: First Delay — Decision to seek care (socioeconomic, cultural, perceived barriers); Second Delay —
Reaching the health facility (distance, transport availability, roads, cost of travel); Third Delay — Receiving
appropriate care at the facility (staff availability, drug stock-outs, waiting times). Urban populations generally
have better physical access but may face other barriers (long queues, impersonal care, cost).
8. Healthcare Provider Factors
The quality of care provided, and the manner of its delivery, profoundly affects future care-seeking
behaviour: Patient-provider communication — poor communication, medical jargon, and failure to explain
diagnosis and treatment plan reduce understanding and adherence; Discrimination and disrespect —
experiences of discrimination based on caste, class, race, gender, or HIV status in health facilities create
lasting barriers to future care-seeking; Waiting times — excessively long waiting times in public facilities
deter patients from returning; Costs — informal payments, excessive charges, and lack of medicines in
public facilities push patients to more expensive private providers or delay care altogether; Continuity of
care — familiarity with a trusted provider increases follow-up and adherence.
9. Media and Information Environment
The media environment shapes public awareness, illness recognition, and care-seeking decisions: Mass
media campaigns (television, radio, print, social media) can significantly increase awareness of disease
symptoms and promote timely care-seeking. Example: HIV testing campaigns, TB DOTS awareness.
Conversely, health misinformation circulated via social media (e.g., COVID-19 vaccine misinformation) can
deter care-seeking and undermine public health programs. Endorsement of healthcare by trusted
community figures (religious leaders, celebrities, community leaders) through media channels is a powerful
facilitator.
10. Religious and Community Norms
For Exam Use Only | Prepared with Gold Standard Content

Health
Religion shapes health beliefs, dietary practices, and attitudes toward biomedical care. Some religious
communities prefer faith healing and prayer over biomedical treatment, causing delays in care-seeking for
serious illness. Religious leaders are key influencers of community health behavior — their endorsement
of vaccination, HIV testing, or family planning has been shown to dramatically increase uptake. Community
gatekeepers (elders, caste leaders, community heads) whose approval is required before health-seeking
behavior can be a facilitator or barrier depending on their attitudes.
MODEL FOR UNDERSTANDING TSB — THE PATHWAY TO CARE
Stage Decision Key Social Factors
Stage 1: Symptom Is this a health problem? Education, health literacy, cultural beliefs
Recognition
Stage 2: Decision to Should I seek care? Perceived severity, SES, gender norms, stigma,
Seek Care family support
Stage 3: Choice of Who should I go to? Cultural beliefs, trust, cost, availability, past
Provider experience
Stage 4: Reaching How do I get there? Distance, transport, cost, time, accompaniment
the Provider
Stage 5: Adherence Will I complete treatment? Understanding, side effects, cost, social support,
to Treatment stigma
PUBLIC HEALTH STRATEGIES TO IMPROVE TSB
● Community health education: Increasing awareness of disease symptoms and importance of early
care.
● ASHA workers and community health workers: Serving as health educators, motivators, and
accompaniers.
● Financial protection: National health insurance schemes (Ayushman Bharat) removing financial
barriers.
● Decentralized services: Sub-centers, PHCs, mobile health units reducing geographic barriers.
● Anti-stigma campaigns: Education to reduce stigma around HIV, TB, mental illness, leprosy.
● Gender-sensitive services: Women-only clinics, female health workers for purdah-observing
communities.
● Task-shifting: Training community health workers for initial contact, reducing specialist waiting
times.
● Telemedicine: Bridging geographic barriers with digital health consultations.
EXAM TIP: For exams, structure your answer using: SES → Education → Culture/Stigma → Gender →
Social Support → Geographic Access → Provider Factors → Media → Religion. This gives you 9
dimensions for a complete essay-type answer.
CONCLUSION: Treatment-seeking behaviour is shaped by a complex interplay of social, cultural,
economic, and health system factors. Improving TSB requires interventions at multiple levels: individual
(education, empowerment), community (reducing stigma, community health workers), and health system
(financial protection, geographic access, respectful care). The ultimate goal is to ensure that every person
who is ill receives timely, appropriate, and quality care.
For Exam Use Only | Prepared with Gold Standard Content

19. STATISTICAL TESTS OF SIGNIFICANCE
Q1. Enumerate Statistical Tests of Significance. How are they useful in Research
Methodology?
INTRODUCTION
Statistical tests of significance are mathematical procedures used to determine whether an observed
difference or association between groups is statistically significant or merely due to chance. They form
the cornerstone of inferential statistics in biomedical research.
FUNDAMENTAL CONCEPTS
Null Hypothesis (H₀):
States there is no real difference or association. Example: H₀: Drug A and Drug B have equal efficacy.
Alternative Hypothesis (H₁/Ha):
States there is a real difference. Can be one-tailed or two-tailed.
p-value:
Probability of obtaining observed results (or more extreme) if H₀ is true. p < 0.05 → statistically significant
(5% level). p < 0.01 → highly significant (1% level). p < 0.001 → very highly significant.
Type I Error (α): Rejecting H₀ when it is actually true (false positive). Conventional α = 0.05.
Type II Error (β): Accepting H₀ when it is actually false (false negative). Power = 1-β.
Confidence Interval (CI): Range of values within which true population parameter lies with specified
confidence (usually 95%).
CLASSIFICATION OF TESTS OF SIGNIFICANCE
A. PARAMETRIC TESTS (require normal distribution assumption)
Test Use Data Type
Student's t-test (unpaired) Compare means of 2 independent groups Continuous, normal data
Student's t-test (paired) Compare means in same group Matched pairs
before/after intervention
One-way ANOVA (F-test) Compare means of ≥3 independent Multiple groups
groups
Two-way ANOVA Effect of 2 factors simultaneously Factorial design
Pearson's correlation (r) Linear association between 2 continuous Bivariate correlation
variables
Simple linear regression Predict one variable from another Prediction model
B. NON-PARAMETRIC TESTS (distribution-free)
Test Use Data Type
Chi-square test (χ²) Association between 2 categorical Nominal/categorical
variables
Mann-Whitney U test Compare 2 independent groups Ordinal/non-normal
Wilcoxon signed-rank test Compare paired groups Matched ordinal data
Kruskal-Wallis test Compare ≥3 groups Non-parametric ANOVA
alternative
Spearman's rank correlation (ρ) Correlation — ordinal data Rank/ordinal

Fisher's exact test Small sample categorical data Small expected frequencies
McNemar's test Paired categorical data Before-after binary
outcomes
C. TESTS FOR SURVIVAL DATA
- Log-rank test: Compare survival curves between groups
- Kaplan-Meier estimator: Estimate survival function
- Cox proportional hazards regression: Multivariate survival analysis
USEFULNESS IN RESEARCH METHODOLOGY
1. HYPOTHESIS TESTING
- Provides objective criteria to accept or reject null hypothesis
- Converts clinical questions into testable statistical hypotheses
- Standardizes decision-making across researchers globally
2. SAMPLE SIZE CALCULATION
- Statistical tests determine required sample size for adequate power (usually 80-90%)
- Prevents underpowered studies (Type II error) and overpowered studies (waste of resources)
3. COMPARISON OF INTERVENTIONS
- RCTs use t-tests, ANOVA to compare treatment groups
- Chi-square tests compare proportions (e.g., cure rates, event rates)
4. EXPLORATION OF ASSOCIATIONS
- Correlation coefficients quantify strength of relationships
- Regression models identify independent predictors while controlling confounders
5. VALIDITY OF DIAGNOSTIC TESTS
- Sensitivity, specificity, PPV, NPV calculated and tested statistically
- ROC curves and area under curve (AUC) with CIs
6. META-ANALYSIS
- Pooled effect sizes with CIs across multiple studies
- Heterogeneity tested by I² statistic and Q-test
7. INTERPRETATION OF CLINICAL SIGNIFICANCE vs STATISTICAL SIGNIFICANCE
- Statistical significance ≠ clinical significance
- Large samples can make trivial differences statistically significant
- Confidence intervals provide clinical context to p-values
EXAM TIP: This is a 5-star repeat question. Always: (1) Define null hypothesis, p-value, errors; (2) Give
classified table of tests; (3) Discuss uses in research. Mentioning 'parametric vs non-parametric' choice
criteria earns bonus marks.
Q2. Discuss the role of Chi-square test in Biomedical Research
INTRODUCTION
The Chi-square (χ²) test, developed by Karl Pearson , is the most commonly used non-parametric
test in biomedical research for analyzing categorical data. It tests whether observed frequencies differ
significantly from expected frequencies.
TYPES OF CHI-SQUARE TEST
Type Use
Chi-square test of independence Tests association between 2 categorical variables
(contingency table analysis)

Chi-square goodness-of-fit test Tests whether observed frequency distribution fits
an expected distribution
McNemar's Chi-square test Tests paired categorical data (before-after studies)
Mantel-Haenszel Chi-square Tests association controlling for confounding
variable (stratified analysis)
FORMULA
χ² = Σ [(O - E)² / E]
Where O = Observed frequency, E = Expected frequency
E = (Row total × Column total) / Grand total
Degrees of freedom (df) = (r-1)(c-1) where r = rows, c = columns
CONDITIONS FOR USE
- Data should be in frequencies (counts), not percentages
- Expected frequency in each cell should be ≥5 (if not, use Fisher's exact test)
- Observations should be independent
- Total sample size should be ≥20
PROCEDURE — 2×2 CONTINGENCY TABLE
Step 1: Set up 2×2 or larger contingency table
Step 2: Calculate expected frequencies for each cell
Step 3: Calculate χ² = Σ[(O-E)²/E]
Step 4: Determine degrees of freedom
Step 5: Compare calculated χ² with table value at appropriate df and significance level
Step 6: If calculated χ² > table value → reject H₀ → statistically significant association
YATES' CORRECTION FOR CONTINUITY
Applied for 2×2 tables when any expected cell frequency is between 5-10:
χ²(corrected) = Σ [(|O - E| - 0.5)² / E]
Reduces the χ² value, making the test more conservative and reducing Type I error.
APPLICATIONS IN BIOMEDICAL RESEARCH
- Comparing disease frequency between exposed and unexposed groups (cohort studies)
- Comparing exposure rates between cases and controls (case-control studies)
- Evaluating vaccine/drug efficacy by comparing outcome proportions
- Testing association between risk factors and disease (e.g., smoking and lung cancer)
- Comparing treatment adherence rates across demographic groups
- Genetic studies: Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium testing
- Diagnostic test evaluation: comparing test results with gold standard
LIMITATIONS
- Cannot determine direction or strength of association (use Cramer's V for strength)
- Cannot be used for continuous data
- Cannot establish causality
- Not suitable for small samples (n<20) or expected cell frequency <5
- Does not indicate which specific category pairs differ (post-hoc tests needed)
EXAM TIP: For Chi-square questions: Always give the formula, Yates' correction, conditions for use,
and at least 5 applications. Fisher's exact test is the alternative for small samples — always mention it.

Q3. Discuss Different Steps in Hypothesis Testing
DEFINITION
Hypothesis testing is a formal statistical procedure to make inferences about a population parameter
based on sample data. It provides a systematic framework for deciding whether the evidence from data
supports or refutes a specific claim about the population.
STEPS IN HYPOTHESIS TESTING
STEP 1: STATE THE HYPOTHESES
- Null Hypothesis (H₀): No difference/association exists (status quo)
- Alternative Hypothesis (H₁): A real difference/association exists
- Two-tailed test: H₁ states difference in either direction
- One-tailed test: H₁ states difference in specific direction
Example: H₀: Mean SBP in intervention group = Mean SBP in control group; H₁: Mean SBP differs
between groups
STEP 2: SET THE SIGNIFICANCE LEVEL (α)
- Pre-determined probability of Type I error
- Conventional levels: α = 0.05 (5%), 0.01 (1%), 0.001 (0.1%)
- Medical research usually uses α = 0.05
- For multiple comparisons, use Bonferroni correction: α/k where k = number of tests
STEP 3: SELECT THE APPROPRIATE STATISTICAL TEST
- Based on: type of data (nominal/ordinal/continuous), number of groups, independent vs paired,
distribution assumption
- Parametric: t-test, ANOVA, correlation for normally distributed continuous data
- Non-parametric: Chi-square, Mann-Whitney for categorical/ordinal/skewed data
STEP 4: COLLECT DATA AND COMPUTE TEST STATISTIC
- Compute the appropriate test statistic (t, F, χ², z, U)
- This converts the observed data into a standardized value
STEP 5: DETERMINE THE p-VALUE OR CRITICAL VALUE
- Compare computed statistic with theoretical distribution (t, F, χ² tables)
- Determine the p-value: probability of getting the observed result if H₀ is true
- If p < α → reject H₀; if p ≥ α → fail to reject H₀
STEP 6: MAKE A STATISTICAL DECISION
Decision Criterion Interpretation
p < α (e.g., p < 0.05) Reject H₀ — Result is statistically significant
p ≥ α (e.g., p ≥ 0.05) Fail to reject H₀ — Result is not statistically
significant
p < 0.01 Highly significant
p < 0.001 Very highly significant
STEP 7: DRAW A CONCLUSION AND INTERPRET IN CONTEXT
- Translate statistical decision into clinical/biological terms
- Report effect size and confidence intervals alongside p-value
- Acknowledge limitations and potential for error
- Statistical significance ≠ clinical significance — always interpret magnitude
ERRORS IN HYPOTHESIS TESTING

Concept Definition
Type I (α) Error Reject true H₀ — False positive — Finding
significance when there is none
Type II (β) Error Accept false H₀ — False negative — Missing real
significance
Power (1-β) Probability of correctly rejecting false H₀ — Aim for
≥80%
Confidence Interval Quantifies uncertainty — more informative than p-
value alone
EXAM TIP: 7-step framework: Hypothesis → Significance level → Test selection → Compute statistic →
p-value → Decision → Conclusion. Always mention Type I/II errors, power, and CI.
Q4. Write about Meta-Analysis and its Application in Medical Research
DEFINITION
Meta-analysis is a statistical technique that combines the results of multiple independent studies
addressing the same research question to produce a single quantitative estimate with greater statistical
power than any individual study. It is the highest level of evidence in the evidence hierarchy (Level Ia —
systematic review with meta-analysis).
PROCEDURE OF META-ANALYSIS
4. Define research question (PICO format: Population, Intervention, Comparison, Outcome)
5. Systematic literature search (PubMed, Cochrane, EMBASE, Google Scholar)
6. Inclusion/exclusion criteria definition and PRISMA flowchart documentation
7. Data extraction and quality assessment (Cochrane Risk of Bias tool, castle-Ottawa Scale)
8. Statistical pooling using appropriate model
9. Assess heterogeneity
10. Perform sensitivity analysis and publication bias assessment
11. Interpret results and draw conclusions
STATISTICAL MODELS
Model Description
Fixed Effects Model Assumes all studies estimate same true effect;
used when I² < 25%; gives more weight to larger
studies; uses inverse-variance method
Random Effects Model (DerSimonian-Laird) Assumes variability in true effects across studies;
used when I² > 25%; more conservative and
appropriate for heterogeneous studies
KEY STATISTICAL MEASURES
- Pooled Effect Estimate: Overall OR, RR, MD, or SMD with 95% CI
- Forest Plot: Visual display of individual and pooled estimates; horizontal lines = CIs; diamond =
pooled effect
- Heterogeneity — I² statistic: 0-25% = low; 26-50% = moderate; >75% = high heterogeneity
- Q-statistic (Cochran's Q): Chi-square test of heterogeneity; significant p suggests heterogeneity
- Funnel Plot: Scatter plot of effect vs precision; asymmetry suggests publication bias
- Egger's test / Begg's test: Formal test for publication bias

APPLICATIONS IN MEDICAL RESEARCH
- Summarizing evidence for clinical guidelines (e.g., WHO, ICMR treatment protocols)
- Resolving conflicting results from individual studies
- Estimating overall effect size when individual studies are underpowered
- Drug efficacy and safety evaluation (pharmacovigilance)
- Health technology assessment for policy decisions
- Identifying gaps in evidence to guide future research
- Comparative effectiveness research
LIMITATIONS
- 'Garbage in, garbage out' — quality of meta-analysis depends on constituent studies
- Publication bias (positive results more likely published)
- Clinical heterogeneity limits meaningful pooling
- Ecological fallacy risk when aggregating individual-level data
EXAM TIP: Meta-analysis question: Must draw/describe a forest plot, mention PRISMA, fixed vs random
effects, I² statistic, funnel plot for publication bias. 'Systematic review + Meta-analysis = Level Ia
evidence' is a quotable line.

20. EUGENICS / EUTHENICS / GENETIC ENGINEERING /
COUNSELLING
Q1 & Q2. Describe / Discuss Eugenics and Euthenics & Their Role in Control of
Genetic Diseases
EUGENICS — DEFINITION
Eugenics (Greek: 'eu' = good; 'genos' = race/birth) is the science of improving a human population by
controlled breeding to increase the occurrence of desirable heritable characteristics. The term was coined
by Sir Francis Galton in 1883.
TYPES OF EUGENICS
A. POSITIVE EUGENICS
Encouraging reproduction among individuals with desirable genetic traits to improve the genetic quality
of the population.
- Incentives for marriage and reproduction among genetically 'fit' individuals
- Education and genetic counseling to promote informed reproductive choices
- Encouraging academically/intellectually gifted individuals to have more children
B. NEGATIVE EUGENICS
Discouraging or preventing reproduction among individuals with undesirable genetic traits.
- Voluntary or involuntary sterilization of individuals with hereditary disorders
- Marriage restrictions for those with genetic disorders
- Segregation in institutions
NOTE: Negative eugenics led to horrific abuses under Nazi Germany (eugenics programs, racial hygiene
laws). It is widely condemned today as pseudoscientific, unethical, and a human rights violation.
HISTORICAL CONTEXT AND ETHICAL ISSUES
- Galton's eugenics was misused to justify racism, colonialism, and genocide
- Forced sterilization programs in USA (Buck v Bell, 1927), Nazi Germany
- Modern genetics has discredited simplistic eugenics — most traits are polygenic and
environmentally influenced
- Modern medical ethics: autonomy, beneficence, non-maleficence oppose coercive eugenics
- Contemporary 'liberal eugenics' — individual choice in reproductive decisions — remains
controversial
ROLE IN CONTROL OF GENETIC DISEASES
- Genetic screening programs identify carriers and affected individuals
- Prenatal diagnosis (amniocentesis, CVS) enables informed reproductive decisions
- Voluntary genetic counseling empowers families without coercion
- Population-based screening for high-frequency disorders (e.g., thalassaemia screening in Cyprus
— 97% reduction in thalassaemic births through voluntary programs)
EUTHENICS — DEFINITION
Euthenics (Greek: 'eu' = good; 'thenos' = strength) is the science of improving human beings by improving
their environment. It was developed as a counter to eugenics, emphasizing that nurture (environment) is
as important as nature (genes) in determining human health and potential.

The term is associated with Ellen H. Richards who emphasized that environmental improvement
— nutrition, sanitation, housing, education — can optimize human development regardless of genetic
endowment.
COMPONENTS OF EUTHENICS
1. NUTRITIONAL ENVIRONMENT
- Adequate nutrition in early life (first 1000 days) prevents stunting, optimizes brain development
- Micronutrient supplementation (iron, folic acid, iodine) prevents neural tube defects, cretinism
- School feeding programs improve cognitive performance
2. PHYSICAL ENVIRONMENT
- Clean water, sanitation (WASH) prevents diarrhoeal diseases and stunting
- Clean air prevents respiratory diseases
- Safe housing prevents injuries and reduces communicable disease transmission
3. SOCIAL ENVIRONMENT
- Education — especially female literacy — is the most powerful euthenicist tool
- Social protection programs reduce poverty-induced deprivation
- Maternal and child health services
4. PSYCHOSOCIAL ENVIRONMENT
- Nurturing caregiving environments optimize neurodevelopment
- Early childhood development programs (ECCE)
COMPARISON: EUGENICS vs EUTHENICS
Aspect EUGENICS EUTHENICS
Focus Genes / Heredity Environment / Nurture
Aim Improve genetic composition of Optimize development through
population environmental improvement
Methods Selective breeding, sterilization, Nutrition, education, sanitation,
genetic testing social programs
Ethics Often coercive and abusive Non-coercive, rights-based
historically approach
Public Health Role Genetic screening, genetic Health promotion, primary
counseling prevention, SDGs
Scientific Basis Oversimplified — traits are Strong evidence base —
polygenic epigenetics supports it
ROLE IN CONTROL OF GENETIC DISEASES
- Eugenics (ethical, voluntary approach): Carrier screening, prenatal diagnosis, preimplantation
genetic testing (PGT) for severe monogenic disorders
- Euthenics: Environmental modification can reduce phenotypic expression of genetic disorders (e.g.,
phenylalanine-free diet for PKU)
- Complementary approach: Genetic counseling (eugenics tool) combined with social support
services (euthenicist approach) is the modern public health model
EXAM TIP: Always write the comparison table. Eugenics: Galton, positive/negative types, ethical
critique. Euthenics: Richards, environmental improvement, nutritional/social interventions.
Thalassaemia example for eugenics in practice.
Q3. Genetic Counseling — Discuss in Detail

DEFINITION
Genetic counseling is the process of helping people understand and adapt to the medical, psychological,
and familial implications of genetic contributions to disease. It involves communication of genetic
information, risk assessment, and provision of support to enable informed decision-making. (ASHG
definition, updated 2006)
OBJECTIVES
12. Establish accurate diagnosis of genetic disorder
13. Estimate recurrence risks for family members
14. Explain available reproductive options and their implications
15. Provide psychological support and facilitate coping
16. Enable autonomous, non-directive informed decision-making
WHO NEEDS GENETIC COUNSELING?
- Couples with family history of genetic disorders (haemophilia, cystic fibrosis, thalassaemia)
- Consanguineous marriages (autosomal recessive risks doubled)
- Advanced maternal age ≥35 years (Down syndrome risk: 1 in 350 at 35, 1 in 100 at 40)
- Previous child with chromosomal anomaly or congenital malformation
- Recurrent miscarriages (≥3) — possible chromosomal translocation
- Couples who are known carriers of genetic conditions
- Exposure to teratogens (radiation, drugs, infections) during pregnancy
- Abnormal prenatal screening results (triple/quadruple test, NIPT)
PROCESS OF GENETIC COUNSELING
Step 1: DIAGNOSIS
- Detailed personal and family history — minimum 3-generation pedigree
- Physical examination, chromosomal analysis (karyotype), molecular genetic tests
- Biochemical tests (enzyme assays for inborn errors of metabolism)
Step 2: RISK ASSESSMENT
- Calculate recurrence risk based on pattern of inheritance
Inheritance Pattern Recurrence Risk
Autosomal Dominant 50% risk for each child
Autosomal Recessive 25% risk if both parents are carriers
X-linked Recessive 50% of sons affected; 50% of daughters are
carriers
Chromosomal (trisomy) 1-2% recurrence risk above age-specific baseline
Multifactorial Empiric recurrence risk from population data
Step 3: COMMUNICATION
- Non-directive counseling — present options without imposing views
- Use simple language, visual aids (pedigree diagrams)
- Allow questions and ensure comprehension
- Written summary of discussion provided
Step 4: DISCUSSION OF OPTIONS
- Prenatal diagnosis: Amniocentesis (16-20 weeks), Chorionic Villus Sampling — CVS (10-13
weeks), NIPT (Non-invasive prenatal testing) from 10 weeks
- Preimplantation Genetic Testing (PGT) with IVF
- Accepting risk and proceeding with pregnancy

- Adoption or use of donor gametes
- Refraining from reproduction
Step 5: PSYCHOLOGICAL SUPPORT
- Address guilt, anxiety, denial, grief
- Refer to support groups and mental health services
- Follow-up counseling sessions
PRINCIPLES OF GENETIC COUNSELING
- Non-directiveness: Counselor facilitates, does not direct reproductive choices
- Confidentiality: Results not disclosed without consent
- Autonomy: Respect for individual and family decision-making
- Informed consent for all genetic testing
- Beneficence and non-maleficence
GENETIC COUNSELING IN INDIA
- Integrated into National Health Mission (NHM)
- Available at medical colleges, AIIMS, regional genetic centers
- National Institute of Biomedical Genomics (NIBMG) supports genetic research
- Integrated Genetic and Rare Disease Portal
- PCPNDT Act regulates prenatal sex determination to prevent misuse
EXAM TIP: Genetic counseling is a potential long essay. Cover: Definition → Who needs it → Process (5
steps) → Principles (non-directive key term) → India context. Recurrence risk table is very high scoring.
Q4. Human Genome Project — Discuss
INTRODUCTION
The Human Genome Project (HGP) was an international scientific research project with the primary goal
of determining the sequence of nucleotide base pairs that make up human DNA and identifying and
mapping all of the genes of the human genome from both a physical and a functional standpoint.
KEY FACTS
Parameter Detail
Duration 1990–2003 (13 years)
Cost ~US$ 3 billion
Coordinating body National Human Genome Research Institute
(NHGRI), USA + 18 countries
Director James Watson (1990-1992); Francis Collins
(1993-2003)
Private competition Celera Genomics (Craig Venter) — completed
draft in 2001
Publication Draft genome published in Nature & Science,
February 2001; Complete sequence April 2003
Human chromosomes 46 (23 pairs) — ~3.2 billion base pairs
Number of genes ~20,000-25,000 protein-coding genes (less than
expected)
Coding DNA Only 1.5% of genome codes for protein

TECHNOLOGIES USED
- Sanger sequencing (chain termination method) — initial gold standard
- Shotgun sequencing — fragmenting genome and reassembling sequences
- Fluorescence-labeled dideoxy chain terminators
- Automated sequencing machines
- Bioinformatics tools for assembly and analysis
KEY FINDINGS
- Humans have approximately 20,000-25,000 genes (significantly fewer than the 100,000 predicted)
- 98.5% of DNA is non-coding ('junk DNA' — now known to have regulatory functions)
- 99.9% of DNA sequence is identical among all humans
- 0.1% variation accounts for individual differences in appearance, disease susceptibility
- Average gene contains ~27,000 base pairs
- Chromosome 1 has the most genes; Y chromosome the fewest
APPLICATIONS AND SIGNIFICANCE
1. MEDICINE AND DISEASE
- Identification of genes responsible for monogenic disorders (BRCA1/2, CFTR, HTT)
- Understanding complex disease genetics (CVD, diabetes, cancer, schizophrenia)
- Pharmacogenomics: tailoring drug doses to individual genetic profiles (precision medicine)
- Development of gene therapy approaches
- Companion diagnostics for targeted cancer therapies
2. DIAGNOSTICS
- Prenatal genetic testing (NIPT, chromosomal microarray)
- born screening programs expanded
- Carrier testing for autosomal recessive conditions
- Genetic risk assessment for multifactorial diseases
3. BIOTECHNOLOGY
- Production of recombinant therapeutic proteins (insulin, erythropoietin, clotting factors)
- Development of monoclonal antibodies and biologic drugs
4. FORENSIC SCIENCE
- DNA fingerprinting for identification (Short Tandem Repeat — STR analysis)
- Paternity testing
5. EVOLUTIONARY BIOLOGY AND ANTHROPOLOGY
- Tracing human migration patterns (mitochondrial DNA analysis)
- Comparative genomics with other species
ETHICAL, LEGAL, AND SOCIAL ISSUES (ELSI)
- 15% of HGP funding allocated to ELSI research
- Genetic privacy and discrimination by employers/insurers
- Genetic Nondiscrimination Act (GINA), USA 2008
- Equitable access to genomic medicine
- Psychological impact of genetic risk information on patients
- Consent and ownership of genetic data
- Designer babies and enhancement via gene editing (CRISPR)
FUTURE: POST-GENOMIC ERA
- 1000 Genomes Project: cataloguing human genetic variation

- ENCODE Project: functional annotation of non-coding genome
- Pan-genome: capturing full human genetic diversity
- Next-generation sequencing (NGS): whole genome sequencing for <$1000 now possible
EXAM TIP: HGP: Key dates (1990-2003), key findings (20-25k genes, 1.5% coding), applications
(pharmacogenomics, gene therapy, forensics), ELSI. Mention Craig Venter/Celera competition for extra
credit.

21. SUSTAINABLE DEVELOPMENT GOALS (SDG) / MILLENNIUM
DEVELOPMENT GOALS (MDG)
Q1. Classify SDGs Based on Core Principles. Discuss Health-related Goals
INTRODUCTION TO SDGs
The Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs) are 17 interlinked global goals adopted by all 193 United
Nations member states at the UN Summit in September 2015 through Resolution 70/1 — 'Transforming
Our World: The 2030 Agenda for Sustainable Development.' They succeeded the Millennium
Development Goals (MDGs) and are to be achieved by 2030.
CORE PRINCIPLES OF SDGs — THE 5Ps FRAMEWORK
The 5Ps represent the pillars upon which SDGs are organized:
Principle (5P) SDGs Under This Principle
PEOPLE Eradicate poverty, hunger; achieve health,
education, gender equality (SDGs 1,2,3,4,5)
PLANET Sustainable management of natural resources,
climate action (SDGs 6,12,13,14,15)
PROSPERITY Economic growth, industrialization, infrastructure,
employment (SDGs 7,8,9,10,11)
PEACE Peaceful, inclusive societies, justice, strong
institutions (SDG 16)
PARTNERSHIP Global partnership for sustainable development,
means of implementation (SDG 17)
ALL 17 SUSTAINABLE DEVELOPMENT GOALS
Goal Short Title Description
SDG 1 No Poverty End poverty in all its forms everywhere
SDG 2 Zero Hunger End hunger, food security, improved nutrition, sustainable agriculture
SDG 3 Good Health & Well- Ensure healthy lives and promote well-being for all at all ages
being
SDG 4 Quality Education Inclusive and equitable quality education; lifelong learning
SDG 5 Gender Equality Achieve gender equality; empower all women and girls
SDG 6 Clean Water & Ensure clean water and sanitation for all
Sanitation
SDG 7 Affordable & Clean Access to affordable, reliable, sustainable and modern energy
Energy
SDG 8 Decent Work & Sustained, inclusive and sustainable economic growth
Economic Growth
SDG 9 Industry, Innovation & Resilient infrastructure; inclusive and sustainable industrialization
Infrastructure
SDG 10 Reduced Inequalities Reduce inequality within and among countries
SDG 11 Sustainable Cities Inclusive, safe, resilient and sustainable cities

SDG 12 Responsible Sustainable consumption and production patterns
Consumption
SDG 13 Climate Action Take urgent action to combat climate change and its impacts
SDG 14 Life Below Water Conserve and sustainably use oceans, seas, marine resources
SDG 15 Life on Land Protect, restore and promote sustainable use of terrestrial ecosystems
SDG 16 Peace, Justice & Inclusive societies for sustainable development; justice for all
Strong Institutions
SDG 17 Partnerships for the Revitalize global partnership for sustainable development
Goals
SDG 3 — GOOD HEALTH AND WELL-BEING (The Primary Health Goal)
SDG 3 has 13 Targets and 28 Indicators. Key Targets:
Target Description
Target 3.1 Reduce global maternal mortality ratio to <70 per
100,000 live births by 2030
Target 3.2 End preventable deaths of borns and children
under 5 — NMR <12; U5MR <25 per 1,000 live
births
Target 3.3 End AIDS, TB, malaria, NTDs, hepatitis, water-
borne diseases, other communicable diseases
Target 3.4 Reduce premature mortality from NCDs by 1/3
through prevention and treatment; promote mental
health
Target 3.5 Strengthen prevention and treatment of substance
abuse, including narcotic drug abuse
Target 3.6 Halve global deaths and injuries from road traffic
accidents by 2020 (extended to 2030)
Target 3.7 Ensure universal access to sexual and
reproductive healthcare services, including FP
Target 3.8 UNIVERSAL HEALTH COVERAGE (UHC) —
financial risk protection, quality essential services,
safe medicines
Target 3.9 Reduce deaths and illnesses from hazardous
chemicals and air, water, soil pollution
Target 3.a Strengthen tobacco control (WHO FCTC
implementation)
Target 3.b Medicines and vaccines for developing countries
— TRIPS flexibilities, Doha Declaration
Target 3.c Substantially increase health financing and health
workforce in developing countries
Target 3.d Strengthen capacity for early warning, risk
reduction for national and global health risks
OTHER HEALTH-RELATED SDGs
- SDG 1 (No Poverty): Financial protection for healthcare; catastrophic health expenditure reduction
- SDG 2 (Zero Hunger): Nutrition targets — end malnutrition, stunting, wasting, micronutrient
deficiencies by 2030

- SDG 5 (Gender Equality): Reproductive rights, end FGM and child marriage
- SDG 6 (Clean Water & Sanitation): WASH prevents 525,000 child deaths/year from diarrhoea
- SDG 10 (Reduced Inequality): Health inequity reduction — key social determinant
- SDG 11 (Sustainable Cities): Healthy urban environments, air quality
- SDG 13 (Climate Action): Climate-sensitive diseases — malaria, dengue, heat stress
EXAM TIP: SDG 3 targets — memorize with mnemonic: 'MCH → CDs → NCDs/Mental → Substances →
Traffic → SRHR → UHC → Pollution → Tobacco → Medicines → Workforce → IHR'. UHC is the key
overarching target.
Q2. Enumerate SDGs. Discuss in Detail on Goal 3
The enumeration of all 17 SDGs is provided in the table above (Q1). Goal 3 is detailed above. Additional
points for a complete essay on Goal 3:
INDIA'S PROGRESS ON SDG 3
Indicator Status
Maternal Mortality Ratio 97/100,000 LB (2018-20) — Below target of <100
achieved; Target: <70 by 2030
Under-5 Mortality Rate 32/1000 LB — Progress towards target of
<25
Neonatal Mortality Rate 20/1000 LB
HIV Prevalence Declining — 0.22% adult prevalence
TB Incidence 210/100,000 — TARGET: End TB by 2025
(National TB Elimination Program)
NCD Mortality CVD, Diabetes, Cancer rising — challenge for
Target 3.4
UHC Index India score: 61/100 — Ayushman Bharat
key initiative
Tobacco Control COTPA 2003, tobacco tax increases, graphic
warnings
KEY PROGRAMS SUPPORTING SDG 3 IN INDIA
- Ayushman Bharat PM-JAY: World's largest health insurance scheme — Rs 5 lakh/family/year for
107 million families → UHC
- Health and Wellness Centers (HWCs): 1,50,000 SHCs upgraded for comprehensive primary care
- National TB Elimination Program (NTEP): Target to eliminate TB by 2025 (5 years before global
2030 target)
- Mission Indradhanush: Vaccination coverage expansion — target 90% full immunization coverage
- Poshan Abhiyaan (National Nutrition Mission): Reduce stunting/wasting/undernutrition and
anaemia
- NHM (National Health Mission): RMNCH+A strategy for reproductive, maternal, neonatal, child
health
- National Mental Health Program (NMHP): Addressing Target 3.4 for mental health
Q3. List SDGs for 2030. Differences between SDG and MDG
The 17 SDGs are listed in the table above. Here is the comprehensive comparison between MDGs and
SDGs:

MILLENNIUM DEVELOPMENT GOALS (MDGs) — OVERVIEW
- Adopted at UN Millennium Summit, September 2000; duration 2000-2015
- 8 goals with 21 targets and 60 indicators
- Focused primarily on developing countries — asymmetric obligations
MDG HEALTH-RELATED GOALS
Goal Health Relevance
MDG 1 Eradicate extreme hunger and poverty (Target:
Halve proportion of undernourished by 2015)
MDG 4 Reduce child mortality (Target: Reduce U5MR by
2/3 between 1990-2015)
MDG 5 Improve maternal health (Target: Reduce MMR by
3/4; universal access to reproductive health)
MDG 6 Combat HIV/AIDS, malaria, and other diseases
MDG 7 Ensure environmental sustainability (Target: Halve
proportion without safe water/sanitation)
MDGs vs SDGs — COMPREHENSIVE COMPARISON
Criterion MDGs SDGs
Number of Goals 8 Goals, 21 Targets 17 Goals, 169 Targets, 232 Indicators
Period 2000–2015 2015–2030
Scope Developing countries only Universal — all countries
Health focus 4 goals partially/fully health-related 17 goals — all interconnected with
health
Origin/Process Top-down — negotiated by Bottom-up — extensive consultation
governments with limited public input including civil society
Economic dimension Growth assumed; inequalities not Explicit focus on reducing inequalities
addressed (SDG 10)
Environmental dimension MDG 7 partly addresses it 5 dedicated environmental goals
(6,12,13,14,15)
Health goal MDGs 4, 5, 6 (separate) SDG 3 — comprehensive single goal
with 13 targets
NCDs Not specifically addressed Target 3.4 explicitly addresses NCDs
and mental health
UHC Not mentioned Target 3.8 — UHC is a central pillar
Gender MDG 3 — limited to education equality SDG 5 — comprehensive gender
equality and empowerment
Governance Not addressed SDG 16 — dedicated to peace, justice,
strong institutions
Means of Implementation MDG 8 — aid and trade (limited) SDG 17 — comprehensive multi-
stakeholder partnerships
Monitoring 60 indicators, limited data systems 232 indicators; open data; national
reporting frameworks
India's achievements on MMR reduced 77% (254→97); IMR India among top SDG performers in
MDGs reduced; malaria controlled Asia for some indicators

ACHIEVEMENT OF MDGs GLOBALLY
- Extreme poverty halved (MDG 1) — 700 million lifted from extreme poverty
- Under-5 mortality reduced by 53% (MDG 4 — partially achieved)
- Maternal mortality fell by 45% (MDG 5 — partially achieved)
- 15 million lives saved from HIV/AIDS (MDG 6)
- 2.6 billion gained access to clean water (MDG 7)
- Unfinished agenda from MDGs embedded in SDGs
CRITICISMS OF MDGs ADDRESSED BY SDGs
- MDGs were prescriptive from developed countries (SDGs are universally applicable)
- MDGs did not address structural causes of poverty (SDGs include inequalities, governance)
- MDGs focused on averages — masked inequalities (SDGs focus on leaving no one behind)
- No attention to climate change in MDGs (SDGs have dedicated climate goal)
- MDGs mainly health targets focused on communicable diseases (SDGs include NCDs)
EXAM TIP: For MDG vs SDG: Always use a comparison table. Key differences to highlight: Universal vs
developing world; 8 vs 17 goals; MDG health goals (4,5,6) vs SDG 3 comprehensive; UHC absent in
MDGs, central in SDGs; 'Leave No One Behind' is the SDG principle. Mention India's SDG ranking.

QUICK REVISION — HIGH-YIELD FACTS
Topic 18: Determinants of Health
Key Concept Key Fact
Dahlgren & Whitehead Model Rainbow model — 5 concentric layers
WHO CSDH Sir Michael Marmot, 2005-2008 — 3 pillars
COVID-19 NPIs Mask, Distance, Wash hands, Ventilate, Stay
home (MDWVS)
Social determinants Income, Education, Employment, Housing, Food,
Discrimination
Urban Health Paradox Urban areas have infrastructure but slums have
worse health than rural
SDH definition Conditions in which people are born, grow, live,
work, age (WHO)
Topic 19: Statistical Tests
Parameter Value/Description
p < 0.05 Statistically significant (5% level)
Chi-square formula Σ(O-E)²/E; df = (r-1)(c-1)
Yates' correction |(O-E)| - 0.5 before squaring; for expected cell 5-
10
Fisher's exact test Expected cell frequency <5; small samples
I² statistic >75% = high heterogeneity in meta-analysis
Type I error Reject true H₀ (false positive) = α
Fixed vs Random effects Fixed: I²<25%; Random: I²>25%
Topic 20: Eugenics / Genetics
Key Concept Key Fact
Eugenics coined by Francis Galton, 1883
Euthenics coined by Ellen H. Richards, 1910
AR recurrence risk (both carriers) 25%
AD recurrence risk 50%
Down syndrome risk age 35 1 in 350 (vs 1 in 1500 at age 20)
Amniocentesis timing 16-20 weeks
CVS timing 10-13 weeks
HGP duration and cost 1990-2003; US$ 3 billion; ~20-25k genes
Topic 21: SDG/MDG
Key Concept Key Fact
SDGs adopted September 2015; 2015-2030; 17 goals, 169
targets

MDGs adopted September 2000; 2000-2015; 8 goals, 21 targets
SDG 3 key target 3.8: Universal Health Coverage (UHC)
MMR target (SDG 3.1) <70 per 100,000 live births by 2030
U5MR target (SDG 3.2) <25 per 1,000 live births by 2030
India's MMR (2018-20) 97 per 100,000 live births
5Ps of SDGs People, Planet, Prosperity, Peace, Partnership
SDG principle 'Leave No One Behind' — universal, inclusive
India TB target Eliminate TB by 2025 (ahead of global 2030 target)
Dr. NTR University of Health Sciences | Topics 18–21 | Paper I — SPM

COMMUNITY MEDICINE
Gold Medal Essay Answers
NTRUHS Postgraduate Theory Examination
Topics: Health Promotion | Maslow's Hierarchy | ICD-11 | Iceberg Phenomenon | Case-Control /
Cohort / RCT Studies
Each answer includes: Introduction • Definitions • Core Content • Recent Updates • Mnemonics

subsections.title: 14. HEALTH PROMOTION — Applications & Concepts
subsections.content:
Q1. Applications of Health Promotion [Oct 2025 — ]
Health promotion is one of the cornerstones of modern public health practice. As populations
increasingly face complex, multi-factorial determinants of disease, health promotion offers a
comprehensive framework that goes beyond mere disease prevention to actively enhance the quality
of life of individuals and communities.
Definition
The Ottawa Charter (1986, WHO) defines Health Promotion as 'the process of enabling people to
increase control over and to improve their health.' It is not limited to clinical settings but encompasses
social, environmental, and policy dimensions.
Prerequisites for Health (Ottawa Charter)
- Peace
- Shelter
- Education
- Food
- Income
- A stable ecosystem
- Sustainable resources
- Social justice and equity

Five Action Areas of Health Promotion (Ottawa Charter)
- 1. Build Healthy Public Policy — Integrating health considerations into all sectors (e.g.,
tobacco taxation, food labeling)
- 2. Create Supportive Environments — Physical and social environments that support health
(e.g., safe housing, clean water)
- 3. Strengthen Community Action — Empowering communities through Participatory
Learning and Action (PLA)
- 4. Develop Personal Skills — Health education, life skills training, and capacity building
- 5. Reorient Health Services — Shifting focus from curative to preventive and promotive care
Applications of Health Promotion
Health promotion is applied across multiple settings and populations:
- School Health Programs: Life skills education, adolescent sexual health, mental wellness
sessions
- Worksite Health Promotion: Ergonomic interventions, stress management, anti-tobacco
drives
- Hospital-Based Promotion: Patient counseling, discharge planning, chronic disease self-
management
- Community-Level Campaigns: Pulse polio, Swachh Bharat, National Health Mission
- Digital Health Promotion: Social media campaigns, mHealth apps, telemedicine counseling
- Policy Applications: FSSAI food regulations, Mental Healthcare Act 2017, National Tobacco
Control Programme
Modern Approaches
The Bangkok Charter extended health promotion to address globalization. It emphasized
advocacy, investment, capacity building, regulation, and partnerships. The Sustainable Development
Goals embed health promotion principles in Goal 3 (Good Health and Well-being).
Health Promotion vs Disease Prevention
- Health Promotion: Positive, proactive, focuses on well-being; addresses all determinants;
empowers individuals
- Disease Prevention: Reactive, focuses on reducing risk; targets specific diseases; medically
oriented
📌 Exam Tip: Questions often ask to 'distinguish health promotion from disease prevention' —
emphasize the Ottawa Charter's empowerment model.
🧠 MNEMONIC — Health Promotion Action Areas — 'BCSDR'
B — Build Healthy Public Policy

C — Create Supportive Environments
S — Strengthen Community Action
D — Develop Personal Skills
R — Reorient Health Services
Memory hook: 'Be Careful, Students Do Revision'
Q2. Community Involvement in Health Decisions [Dec 2024 — ]
Community participation is the sine qua non of effective health promotion. The shift from a top-down
medical model to a bottom-up, participatory model has been a defining feature of modern public
health practice since the Declaration of Alma-Ata .
Levels of Community Participation (Arnstein's Ladder)
- Level 1 — Manipulation: Authorities using community as rubber stamp
- Level 2 — Therapy: Engaging community without real power
- Level 3 — Informing: One-way communication
- Level 4 — Consultation: Community opinions sought but not necessarily used
- Level 5 — Placation: Community advises but power remains with authorities
- Level 6 — Partnership: Negotiated planning and decision-making
- Level 7 — Delegated Power: Community has majority power
- Level 8 — Citizen Control: Full community control
Methods to Enhance Community Involvement
- Participatory Learning and Action (PLA): Rapid rural appraisal, transect walks, social
mapping
- Village Health Sanitation and Nutrition Committees (VHSNCs): Under NHM, empowers
village-level decision-making
- Gram Sabhas: Democratic platforms for local health planning
- ASHA Workers: Community health workers as bridges between communities and health
systems
- Community Advisory Boards: In research and program planning
- Focus Group Discussions (FGDs): For needs assessment and program evaluation
Barriers to Community Participation
- Low health literacy and education
- Social inequities — caste, gender, religion
- Lack of trust in health systems

- Political disempowerment
- Language and communication gaps
Success Stories
The SEARCH (Society for Education, Action and Research in Community Health) project in Gadchiroli
is a landmark example of community-led health decision-making reducing neonatal mortality by over
70% through participatory approaches.
🧠 MNEMONIC — Community Participation Methods — 'PVGAF'
P — PLA (Participatory Learning and Action)
V — VHSNCs (Village Health Sanitation Committees)
G — Gram Sabhas
A — ASHA Workers
F — Focus Group Discussions
Memory hook: 'People Value Good And Fair participation'
Q3. Participatory Learning and Action (PLA) in Public Health [May
2025 — ]
Participatory Learning and Action (PLA) is an evolving family of approaches and methods aimed at
enabling local people to share, enhance, and analyze their knowledge of life and conditions, to plan
and to act. It evolved from Rapid Rural Appraisal (RRA) and is now widely adopted in community
health programs globally.
Key Features of PLA
- Participatory: Communities are co-researchers and co-decision makers
- Empowerment-focused: Builds local capacities and confidence
- Visual methods: Mapping, ranking, seasonal calendars reduce literacy barriers
- Iterative: Continuous cycles of learning, reflection, and action
Core PLA Techniques
- Social/Resource Mapping: Communities map their health resources (hospitals, water
sources, roads)
- Seasonal Calendars: Identifying disease patterns by season (e.g., malaria in monsoons)
- Transect Walks: Walking through communities to observe health hazards systematically
- Ranking and Scoring: Priority ranking of health problems (problem ranking, pairwise ranking)
- Venn Diagrams: Mapping relationships between community institutions

- Focus Group Discussions: Group exploration of health perceptions and needs
- Participatory Epidemiology: Community-generated disease maps and case reporting
Applications in Public Health
- Maternal & Child Health: SNEHA program (Mumbai) used PLA with women's groups to
reduce neonatal mortality
- WATSAN Programs: PLA for community-led Total Sanitation (CLTS)
- Malaria Control: Community mapping of breeding sites
- HIV/AIDS Programs: Stigma mapping, participatory needs assessment
- Health System Strengthening: Community scorecards for health service quality
Evidence Base
Manandhar et al. (Lancet, 2004) demonstrated that women's group PLA in Nepal reduced neonatal
mortality by 30% and maternal mortality by 78% — one of the most significant community health
intervention outcomes documented.
📌 Exam Tip: PLA questions commonly appear in context of NHM and community health workers — link
to ASHA, VHSNC, and sub-centre level activities.
🧠 MNEMONIC — PLA Techniques — 'SMART VF'
S — Seasonal Calendars
M — Mapping (Social/Resource)
A — Analytical Ranking
R — Resource Flow Diagrams
T — Transect Walks
V — Venn Diagrams
F — Focus Group Discussions
Memory hook: 'SMART communities use Very Fair methods'

subsections.title: 15. MASLOW'S HIERARCHY / BEHAVIORAL THEORIES
subsections.content:
Q1. Maslow's Hierarchy of Needs Theory [Oct 2025 — ]
Abraham Maslow's Hierarchy of Needs (1943, 'A Theory of Human Motivation') is a foundational
motivational theory in psychology and public health. It proposes that human behavior is driven by a
hierarchical set of needs, and that lower-order needs must be substantially satisfied before higher-
order needs become motivating.
The Five-Level Hierarchy (Classical Model)

- Level 1 — PHYSIOLOGICAL NEEDS (Deficiency Needs): Air, water, food, shelter, sleep,
warmth, clothing. These are survival needs and the most prepotent (powerful). Example: A
hungry patient cannot focus on health education.
- Level 2 — SAFETY NEEDS (Deficiency Needs): Physical safety, job security, financial
security, health, property. Example: Communities in conflict zones prioritize safety over
disease prevention.
- Level 3 — LOVE & BELONGING (Deficiency Needs): Friendship, intimacy, family, sense of
community. Example: Social isolation during COVID-19 impacted mental health significantly.
- Level 4 — ESTEEM NEEDS (Deficiency Needs): Self-esteem, achievement, recognition,
status, respect. Example: Patients with chronic illness may have impaired self-esteem.
- Level 5 — SELF-ACTUALIZATION (Growth Need): Achieving full potential, personal growth,
peak experiences. Example: Motivated health workers pursuing academic excellence.
Extended Hierarchy (Maslow 1970 — 8-level model)
- Added: Cognitive Needs (knowledge, curiosity) — between Esteem and Self-actualization
- Added: Aesthetic Needs (beauty, order) — above Cognitive
- Added: Transcendence (helping others self-actualize) — above Self-actualization
Applications in Public Health & Community Medicine
- Health Education: Tailor messages to unmet needs — starving populations cannot prioritize
health literacy
- Program Planning: NHM addresses physiological (nutrition programs) → safety (JSSK, safe
delivery) → belonging (ASHA support groups) in sequence
- Patient Counseling: Chronically ill patients may have unmet safety/esteem needs —
address holistically
- Occupational Health: Motivating health workers using recognition, autonomy (higher needs)
- Mental Health: Loneliness (unmet belonging need) is a primary driver of depression
- Health Equity: Marginalized communities stuck at lower-hierarchy levels cannot access self-
actualization health behaviors
Criticisms
- Hierarchy is not always sequential — individuals may seek self-actualization despite unmet
lower needs
- Cultural variation: Collectivist cultures may prioritize belonging over safety
- Limited empirical evidence supporting strict hierarchy
- Later theorists (Alderfer's ERG Theory) compressed it to 3 levels: Existence, Relatedness,
Growth

🧠 MNEMONIC — Maslow's 5 Levels — 'Please Send Love Each Saturday'
P — Physiological
S — Safety
L — Love & Belonging
E — Esteem
S — Self-Actualization
Or: 'Pandas Sleep Like Enormous Snakes'
(bottom to top: Physiological → Safety → Love → Esteem → Self-Actualization)
Q2. JOHARI Window — Describe and Its Applications [May 2025 —
]
The Johari Window is a psychological model developed by Joseph Luft and Harrington Ingham
— 'Jo-hari' from the first names of its creators. It is a framework for understanding interpersonal
communication, self-awareness, and group dynamics, with significant applications in health
communication and team management.
The Four Quadrants of Johari Window
- Quadrant 1 — OPEN/ARENA (Known to self, known to others): Information freely shared.
Example: A doctor's published research, known clinical skills. This area should be
maximized for effective communication.
- Quadrant 2 — BLIND SPOT (Unknown to self, known to others): Others see things about
you that you don't. Example: A clinician unaware of their dismissive communication style; a
patient's observable symptoms they haven't noticed. Feedback reduces this area.
- Quadrant 3 — HIDDEN/FACADE (Known to self, unknown to others): Deliberately
concealed information. Example: A patient hiding substance use from their doctor. Self-
disclosure reduces this area.
- Quadrant 4 — UNKNOWN (Unknown to self, unknown to others): Unexplored potential,
unconscious drives. Example: Latent clinical skills, undiscovered personality traits.
Reflection and therapy unlock this.
Applications in Community Medicine & Public Health
- Doctor-Patient Communication: Expanding the Open quadrant improves therapeutic alliance
and adherence
- Team Management: Used in health team development; reduces Blind Spots through 360-
degree feedback
- Health Education: Identifying Hidden knowledge (patients know risks but hide behavior)
guides motivational interviewing

- Community Needs Assessment: Unknown quadrant represents unrecognized community
health needs — needs participatory methods to uncover
- Leadership Development: PHC Medical Officers use Johari Window in self-improvement
workshops
- Mental Health Counseling: Core tool in psychotherapy for improving self-awareness
Strategies to Expand the Open Window
- Self-disclosure: Sharing relevant personal information
- Soliciting feedback: Structured 360-degree peer review
- Active listening and reflection
- Group process sessions: Team workshops and debriefs
🧠 MNEMONIC — Johari Window — 'OBHiU'
O — Open (known self, known others)
B — Blind Spot (unknown self, known others)
Hi — Hidden/Facade (known self, unknown others)
U — Unknown (unknown self, unknown others)
Memory hook: 'Only Brilliant Hidden Unknowns grow with feedback'
Or: Jo-Hari = Joseph + Harrington (creators)
Q3. Types and Management of Conflict [May 2025 — ]
Conflict is an inevitable feature of organizational and community life. In healthcare settings,
unmanaged conflict reduces team effectiveness, patient safety, and staff morale. Understanding
conflict types and resolution strategies is a critical leadership competency for public health
professionals.
Definition
Conflict is defined as 'a process that begins when one party perceives that another party has
negatively affected, or is about to negatively affect, something that the first party cares about'
(Thomas, 1992).
Types of Conflict
- 1. Intrapersonal Conflict: Within an individual (role conflict, approach-avoidance). Example:
A health worker torn between family duties and night duty calls.
- 2. Interpersonal Conflict: Between two individuals. Example: Disagreement between a
doctor and nurse over patient management.

- 3. Intragroup Conflict: Within a team. Example: PHC staff disagreement on prioritizing
outreach vs. OPD.
- 4. Intergroup Conflict: Between departments or organizations. Example: Conflict between
health and sanitation departments over resource allocation.
- 5. Organizational Conflict: Structural conflicts within the health system. Example: Vertical vs.
horizontal program management disagreements.
Sources of Conflict in Healthcare
- Resource scarcity — inadequate staff, equipment, budget
- Role ambiguity — unclear responsibilities between cadres
- Communication failures — information gaps between levels
- Value differences — professional vs. community health priorities
- Power and authority disputes
Thomas-Kilmann Conflict Management Styles
- 1. Competing (High Assertiveness, Low Cooperation): Win-lose. Used in emergency
situations requiring quick decisions.
- 2. Collaborating (High Assertiveness, High Cooperation): Win-win. Ideal for complex
problems with creative solutions.
- 3. Compromising (Medium both): Give-and-take. Used when time is limited and stakes are
moderate.
- 4. Avoiding (Low both): Withdrawing. Used when issue is trivial or emotions are high.
- 5. Accommodating (Low Assertiveness, High Cooperation): Lose-win. Used to preserve
relationships when wrong.
Conflict Resolution Strategies
- Negotiation: Direct dialogue between parties to reach agreement
- Mediation: Neutral third party facilitates resolution
- Arbitration: Binding decision by an authority
- Structural approaches: Redesigning roles, reporting structures, resource allocation
- Communication improvement: Regular team meetings, open-door policy
🧠 MNEMONIC — Conflict Management Styles — 'C3-AA'
C — Competing
C — Collaborating (best for complex issues)
C — Compromising
A — Avoiding
A — Accommodating

Memory hook: '3 Cs then 2 As — like a school grade C, C, C, A, A'
Winning combo: Collaborate first, Compromise if needed

subsections.title: 16. ICD-11 / CLASSIFICATION OF DISEASES
subsections.content:
Q1. Structure, Features, and Elements of ICD-11 [May
2025 — ]
The International Classification of Diseases, 11th Revision (ICD-11) was adopted by the World Health
Assembly in May 2019 and came into force on January 1, 2022. It represents the most comprehensive
overhaul of disease classification in decades, with transformative implications for global health data
systems, clinical coding, and epidemiological research.
Historical Background
- ICD-1 introduced in 1900 (Bertillon Classification)
- ICD-10 adopted in 1990, in use for over 30 years
- ICD-11: Developed over 10 years with input from 90+ countries and 10,000+ clinicians
- India transitioned to ICD-11 reporting from 2022 onwards
Structure of ICD-11
- 26 Chapters (vs. 22 in ICD-10), covering all disease domains
- Entirely digital — browser-based (ICD-11 Browser), API-accessible
- Coded with alphanumeric system: Letter + 2 digits + dot + 2 digits (e.g., CA24.0)
- Multiple Parenting: Conditions can appear in multiple chapters without duplication
- Linearizations: Foundation Component (comprehensive) + Linearizations for specific uses
(morbidity, mortality)
- Extension Codes: Allow granular specification (severity, laterality, temporality,
histopathology)
Features and Elements
- Digital-first design: Integrated with electronic health records (EHR), compatible with
SNOMED CT and LOINC
- Cluster Coding: Combining codes to fully describe complex conditions (post-coordination)
- Traditional Medicine Chapter (Chapter 26): First-ever inclusion of traditional medicine
(Ayurveda, Chinese medicine)
- Chapter on Sexual Health: Separates sexual health from mental disorders
(homosexuality fully removed since ICD-10)

- Gaming Disorder (6C51): Classified under addictive behaviors — public health relevance
- Bodily Distress Disorder: Replaces somatoform disorders
- CPTSD (Complex PTSD): distinct entity
- Prolonged Grief Disorder: mental health entity
- Gender Incongruence: Moved from mental disorders to sexual health chapter — major
destigmatization step
- Burn-out (QD85): Officially classified as occupational phenomenon
Advantages over ICD-10
- 10x more diagnostic detail (55,000+ codes vs. ~14,400 in ICD-10)
- Reduces coding errors via digital decision support
- Better captures multi-morbidity through cluster coding
- Supports clinical decision support systems (CDSS)
- Improved comparability of international health data
Morbidity Indicators
- Incidence Rate: cases per population per time
- Prevalence Rate: All cases per population at a point in time
- Attack Rate: Cases during outbreak / population at risk × 100
- Case Fatality Rate (CFR): Deaths among cases × 100
- Hospital Admission Rates: Burden indicator in ICD-coded data
📌 Exam Tip: ICD-11 questions may ask about specific entities — memorize: Traditional Medicine
Chapter, Gaming Disorder, Gender Incongruence, Prolonged Grief Disorder, Burn-out.
🧠 MNEMONIC — ICD-11 Highlights — 'DIGITAL'
D — Digital-first (browser, API, EHR integration)
I — International Traditional Medicine Chapter added
G — Gaming Disorder included
I — Includes 55,000+ codes (10× ICD-10)
T — Traditional Medicine Chapter 26
A — API-accessible cluster coding
L — Linearizations for morbidity/mortality
Memory hook: ICD-11 is DIGITAL — because it truly is!

subsections.title: 17. ICEBERG PHENOMENON
subsections.content:
Q1. Iceberg Phenomenon and Its Application in Community Medicine

The Iceberg Phenomenon is one of the most fundamental concepts in epidemiology and community
medicine. Just as an iceberg has only a small visible portion above water with the vast bulk hidden
below the surface, most diseases in a community exist in a subclinical, undiagnosed, or unrecognized
state — with only a fraction becoming clinically apparent.
Definition
The Iceberg Phenomenon states that for most diseases, clinical cases (visible above the 'waterline')
represent only the tip of the total disease burden, while a much larger proportion exists as subclinical
infections, inapparent cases, and unrecognized disease in the community.
Components of the Disease Iceberg
- Above the waterline (Diagnosed/Reported Cases): Cases seeking care; officially counted in
surveillance; represent prevalence as measured
- Below the waterline — Level 1 (Symptomatic but NOT seeking care): Fear, stigma, lack of
access, cost barriers prevent care-seeking
- Below the waterline — Level 2 (Subclinical/Inapparent Disease): Infection present,
pathological changes occurring, but no symptoms yet
- Below the waterline — Level 3 (Pre-pathogenesis/Susceptible Stage): No disease yet, but
risk factors and susceptibility present
Classic Examples
- Diabetes Mellitus: For every diagnosed case, 2-3 undiagnosed cases exist in the community
(IDF data)
- Hypertension: Awareness-treatment-control cascade shows massive iceberg — only ~12%
controlled in India (NFHS-5)
- Tuberculosis: Estimated 2.8 million TB cases in India ; only 2.4 million notified —
significant iceberg
- Poliomyelitis: For every 1 case of paralytic polio, ~200 subclinical infections occur (200:1
ratio)
- Sexually Transmitted Infections: Majority of chlamydia, HSV cases are asymptomatic
- Mental Health: ~10-15% prevalence in community; majority undiagnosed and untreated
Differences Between Incidence and Prevalence in Context of Iceberg
- Incidence: cases per population per time — measures the 'rate of disease'

- Prevalence: All existing cases at a time point — includes BOTH tip and visible part of
iceberg
- The iceberg is best captured by prevalence surveys (community-based), not routine
incidence reporting (facility-based)
- Point prevalence vs. period prevalence — both detect more of the iceberg than incidence
alone
Implications for Health Care Planning
- Screening Programs: Mass screening (NPCDCS, RBSK) to detect submerged portion
- Resource Allocation: Disease burden underestimated by clinical data alone; surveys reveal
true need
- Health System Design: Primary care must be strengthened to surface the iceberg
- Surveillance Enhancement: Passive surveillance (facility-based) misses iceberg; active and
sentinel surveillance needed
- Community Outreach: Reduce barriers to care-seeking through IEC, camps, mobile units
🧠 MNEMONIC — Iceberg Layers — '4 D's below the waterline'
D — Diagnosed cases (ABOVE water)
D — Denied/hidden cases (symptomatic, not seeking care)
D — Dormant/subclinical cases (infected, asymptomatic)
D — Downstream susceptibles (at risk, not yet infected)
Memory hook: 'Disease Drowns Deep Down' — 4 D's of the iceberg
Polio ratio: 1 paralytic : 200 subclinical = a very deep iceberg!
Q2. Describe Iceberg Phenomenon — Differences Between Incidence and
Prevalence
(Core content covered above in Q1. Supplement with the following incidence-prevalence
comparison:)
Incidence vs. Prevalence — Detailed Comparison
- Incidence Rate: Number of cases of disease occurring in a defined population during a
specified time period / Population at risk × K. Measures: Risk of developing disease. Use:
Studying etiology and risk factors. Affected by: Duration of illness.
- Prevalence Rate: Number of EXISTING cases ( + old) at a given point/period / Total
population × K. Measures: Disease burden and magnitude. Use: Health service planning.
Affected by: Incidence and duration.
- Relationship: Prevalence ≈ Incidence × Mean Duration of Disease (for stable conditions)

- In Iceberg context: Prevalence surveys reveal more of the iceberg than incidence reporting
alone, as they capture long-standing undiagnosed cases
🧠 MNEMONIC — Incidence vs Prevalence — ' vs ALL'
Incidence = cases only — measures RISK
Prevalence = ALL cases ( + old) — measures BURDEN
I = Incidence = 'I just got sick'
P = Prevalence = 'Pool of all sick people' (all existing)
Formula link: P ≈ I × D (Prevalence ≈ Incidence × Duration)
Q3. Implications of Iceberg Phenomenon on Planning Health Care Services

The iceberg phenomenon has profound implications for how health services are planned, funded,
and delivered. If health planners rely only on clinical/facility data (the visible tip), they will
systematically underestimate disease burden, misallocate resources, and fail to prevent disease
progression in the subclinical majority.
Planning Implications — Detailed Analysis
- 1. Screening and Early Detection: NPCDCS (Non-Communicable Disease Control
Programme) screens for DM, HTN, cancers at PHC level. RBSK (Rashtriya Bal Swasthya
Karyakram) screens children for 30+ conditions. Objective: Surface the submerged portion
of the iceberg early.
